metaInformation.twitterTitle: Why Women’s Mental Health Needs More Than Therapy: Tia’s Whole-Body Approach

children.text: Mental health
is health. Full stop.
Somewhere along the way, the medical community got in the habit of talking about and treating physical health and mental health as separate entities.
Thankfully, that seems to be changing.
And now, getting a ‘physical’ from your family doc or going for a ‘check-up’ from your general healthcare provider often includes a mental health ‘check-in’ as well – and that’s a very good thing.

Why? Because physical health and mental health are intertwined and interconnected in amazing ways. And because mental health professionals (and appointment times) are in short supply. And because health insurance often doesn’t pay (or pay enough) to make routine mental health visits a practical option.
This is a big reason why mental health check-ins – either self-administered or administered by your healthcare provider – are so all-important.

A mental health check-in is a proactive approach to maintaining and improving mental health and wellness. Unlike physical health issues, mental health problems can present with more subtle signs and symptoms, which make them easier to overlook and ignore.

The benefits of regular mental health check-ins can’t be overstated. These check-ins are an important form of self-care. They encourage self-awareness and can help you connect with your emotions, gauge your ‘state of mind’, recognize early sign of distress, and take action before issues escalate into more severe mental health problems.
The cool part? You don’t have to wait for an appointment to start the check-in process.

The most important mental health practitioner you’ll ever have is, well, you. So, hopefully this article empowers you to pay more attention to your mental health at home and to advocate for yourself if and when the time comes to
seek professional help
for additional support.
The Benefits of Regular Mental Health Check-Ins
Early Detection and Intervention of Issues
Regular mental health check-ins can help people spot early signs of mental health issues, such as
anxiety

, burnout, and depression. It’s often easier to manage symptoms and prevent escalation of mental health problems when you first notice when something doesn’t feel right. Mental health disorders can be hard to spot – but regular check-ins can cut through the confusion and provide a clearer picture so you have a starting point.
Enhanced Self-Awareness
Taking time to assess mental state can increase self-awareness. This insight fosters better emotional regulation and decision-making, and it can improve overall emotional intelligence too.
Better Coping Mechanisms
Regular self-reflection can help people learn which coping skills and strategies work best for
managing stress
, anger, and sadness. In the modern world of medicine, getting regular mental healthcare can feel impossible, but check-ins can help make the mental health mountain easier to climb.
Improved Relationships

Consistently checking-in with yourself can improve interpersonal relationships. Having a better understanding of your emotional needs makes you better equipped to communicate effectively, show empathy, and manage conflicts with others.
Increased Resilience

Routine mental health check-ins can improve resilience by helping individuals recognize their strengths and weaknesses. Acknowledging past struggles and achievements strengthens coping skills for future challenges.
Better Focus, Productivity, and Performance

When mental health is well-managed, it’s easier to stay focused, motivated, and productive. Periodic check-ins help keep stress and distractions in check, supporting a healthy, balanced approach to work and life.
Prevention of Burnout

For people in high-stress environments, regular mental health check-ins are especially crucial for avoiding burnout. They offer an opportunity to reflect, decompress, and recalibrate, which helps prevent emotional exhaustion.
Need mental health support? Join Tia's membership and schedule an appointment to get checked at the clinic nearest you!
Located in New York, Phoenix, San Francisco, and Los Angeles.
Making a Habit of Mental Health Check-Ins
Conducting regular mental health check-ins doesn’t have to be complicated, and it doesn’t require special equipment or lots of time. Here are some tips to help you make it a habit:
Set aside time

Choose a quiet place, start small, and be mindful for a few moments. Asking general questions is a good place to start: How am I feeling? Emotionally? Physically? What thoughts are on my mind? What words or actions have made me feel stressed or brought me joy lately? It might help to set a weekly reminder for a brief check-in or to combine this exercise with an existing habit you already have – morning coffee, evening bath, etc.
Be patient and pay attention

Making a new habit can take time, so be patient. Over time, you’ll get more comfortable carving out time for yourself. So much of life runs on auto-pilot these days. These check-ins should be pro-active exercises that require attention. It’s also important to incorporate a few more specific questions into your regular check-ins:
Do I feel down? Am I having more negative thoughts about myself and others? Do I have a lack of interest or pleasure in doing things? Am I moving in slow-motion or feeling restless?
Have I noticed any changes (an increase or decrease) in sleep, appetite, or energy levels?
Am I having trouble focusing or concentrating?
Have I been socializing or withdrawing more from others?
Write it down

Journal your thoughts on paper or on your phone. Writing down your thoughts and feelings can help clarify your emotions and track any patterns. Rating your mood (1-10) can also help quantify your emotional state and help you notice any trends in your mental health.
Identify triggers
Reflect on recent events, conversations, or situations that impacted your mood. Understanding your triggers can help you and those around you manage reactions.
Consider coping skills
Assess the ways you’ve been coping with stress, mood, overwhelm, or other challenges. Are your current coping mechanisms healthy or contributing to your issues?
Involve others
Consider involving a trusted friend or family member in your mental health journey. Sharing your check-in process with someone else can provide additional accountability and support.
Get screened
There are several self-assessment tools you can use to get a better read on things. A
mental health screening

is a short set of standardized questions that can help catch more serious mental health conditions on the early side. The PHQ-9 is used for measuring the severity of depression while the GAD-7 can help diagnose anxiety disorders. There are screening tools for substance use disorders, eating disorders, PTSD, and many other problems. Self-assessments can be valuable for picking up mental illnesses, but remember they don’t replace the expertise of a mental health professional or their ability to recognize more subtle signs and symptoms these exams don’t have the ability to assess or treatment plan options that require professional oversight.

Better health starts at home. Same goes for mental health. Regular mental health check-ins are a great place to start, and the benefits of these self-assessments are boundless. They can enlighten and empower and provide self-awareness that can really improve your mental health and wellness in wonderful ways.

These check-ins can also help you recognize issues early on - when it’s easier to intervene, treat, and manage them - and help you recognize when those issues may require professional intervention instead.

Also, it’s important to remember this: If you or someone you know is having thoughts of suicide or self-harm, it’s important to get immediate help. Call 911, get to an emergency department, contact a crisis hotline, or reach out to a friend or family member.

children.text: The next time someone asks - “What’s your gut telling you?” - you might want to listen closely to the answer/to the voice inside your belly.

Why? Because the gut and brain can “talk” to each other through nerves, and there is a growing body of evidence that this bidirectional, back-and-forth communication between your digestive system and your central nervous system can have a major impact on the health of each other.

It might seem weird that your gut has the ability to influence your mental state-of-mind, but if you’ve ever felt “butterflies” or had a “nervous stomach” or gotten a “gut feeling” then you already know there’s a link.

This connection, known as the gut-brain axis, makes some scientific sense too. Research shows there are more nerve cells in your gut than anywhere else in the human body (besides the brain) and more information passes between the brain and digestive system than any other system in the body.
Exploring the Link Between Gut Health and Mental Health

The gut-brain axis refers to the two-way communication network that links the gastrointestinal (GI) system to the brain and central nervous system, and this connection has garnered significant attention in recent years.

Studies have shown that people with depression (and other mental health diagnoses) often have altered gut biomes compared to healthy individuals, and for years, scientists postulated that mental health issues came first and GI problems came next.

But new data and observational research are turning this hypothesis on its head. And now scientists believe that an unhealthy gut can actually contribute to mental health issues and not just be the effect of them.

Research has also demonstrated that probiotics – live microorganisms that can confer health benefits – can have a positive impact on mental health and has been associated with improved cognitive function and reduced depression and anxiety in some people.
The gut-brain connection involves other mechanisms besides the digestive system and nervous system and also includes the immune system, endocrine system, and hormonal pathways.
There are several key players at the heart of this gut-brain relationship:
Enteric Nervous System

The enteric nervous system describes the network of nerves that operate within the GI tract and control digestion. With hundreds of millions of neurons, it’s the most complex ‘nervous system’ outside of the brain, which has led some scientists to refer to it as a ‘second brain’.

This system is part of the autonomic nervous system, which governs the automatic/involuntary functions of your internal organs. This means, while you’re busy doing other things – sleeping, working, playing – the enteric nervous system is continuously gathering and processing information about your GI system and responding without much input from the brain.
The Vagus Nerve

The vagus nerve is a major nerve that runs from the brainstem to the abdomen, and it plays a crucial role in transmitting signals from the enteric nervous system to the brain. This nerve shares sensory information about the gut and receives motor signals from the brain, which allows it to mount a rapid response to chemical changes and the presence of food.
Gut Microbiome

Another key player in the gut-brain connection is the gut microbiome – a complex community of microorganisms, like bacteria, viruses, and fungi, that reside in the digestive tract. These microbes help produce neurotransmitters that convey messages between the gut and brain, and they actively participate in numerous physiological functions, including digestion and immune function.

There is significant overlap between people who have functional gastrointestinal disorders, like IBS (irritable bowel syndrome), and mental health problems, like anxiety, and recent studies show that the gut microbiome likely plays a role in this observation.
Experiencing digestive issues? Join Tia's membership and schedule an appointment to get checked at the clinic nearest you!
Located in New York, Phoenix, San Francisco, and Los Angeles.
How Gut Health Influences Mental Health
The relationship between gut health and mental health is complicated, and researchers are just starting to better understand the connection. Here are a few of the mechanisms involved:
Neurotransmitter Production:

Many of the neurotransmitters critical for mood regulation, such as serotonin, dopamine, and gamma-aminobutyric acid (GABA), are produced in the gut. In fact, approximately 90% of the body's serotonin - a neurotransmitter associated with feelings of well-being and happiness - is produced in the gut.
Inflammation and the Immune System:

Chronic inflammation is increasingly recognized as a contributing factor to various mental health conditions, including depression and anxiety. The gut microbiome helps regulate the body's immune response, and an imbalance in gut bacteria can lead to increased inflammation, impaired brain function, and the development of mental health disorders.
The Gut Barrier and Leaky Gut Syndrome:

The gut lining serves as a barrier that prevents harmful substances, such as toxins and pathogens, from entering the bloodstream. When this barrier is compromised - a condition often referred to as "leaky gut" - it can allow these harmful substances to enter the bloodstream. This process triggers an immune response and inflammation, which can lead to cognitive decline and mental health issues.
Stress Response and the HPA Axis:

The hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal (HPA) axis is a central part of the body's stress response system. Research suggests that the gut microbiome can influence the activity of the HPA axis and affect how the body responds to stress. Unhealthy amounts of stress can cause inflammation and impaired immunity which, in turn, can cause mental health problems.
Steps to Support Gut and Mental Health

Considering the strong connection between gut health and mental health, it pays to have a strategy for supporting and improving your brain and belly health. Experts continue to explore the best ways to nurture the gut-brain connection, but for now, their best advice involves two primary considerations: improving your diet and reducing your stress.
Improving Your Diet

A diet rich in fiber, fruit, vegetables, and fermented food can promote a healthier gut biome. Yogurt, kefir, and kimchi contain gut-friendly probiotics while garlic, onions, and bananas have prebiotics that help feed healthy gut bacteria.
Antioxidants and anti-inflammatory foods, which are found in whole foods, like fruits and vegetables, fight harmful free radicals and can help prevent inflammation.
Limiting the intake of processed food, sugar, and alcohol can help too. These substances can disrupt the gut biome and cause an inflammatory response.
Reducing Stress
Chronic stress has a major impact on gut and brain health, so finding effective ways to manage stress is crucial to maintaining a strong gut-brain connection.
Mindfulness practices, like meditation and yoga, deep breathing exercises, regular exercise, and better sleep are all great ways to reduce stress and improve your gut and mental health.

Try therapy. Cognitive behavioral therapy, gut-directed relaxation training, and biofeedback are all wonderful ways to reduce stress and improve gut health, mental health, and overall health and wellbeing.

The gut-brain axis is a super highway of nerves and chemical messengers that connect our digestive system and central nervous system, and this connection underscores the importance of taking a holistic approach to health and wellbeing. While science continues to explore this complex relationship, we can nurture our gut biomes with lifestyle changes, like eating a healthy diet and reducing stress levels, and improve our gut health, brain health, and overall health and wellbeing in the process.
Need gut health support? Join Tia's membership and schedule an appointment to get checked at the clinic nearest you!
Located in New York, Phoenix, San Francisco, and Los Angeles.

children.text: Yearly Skin Exam

items: A head-to-toe skin check with a specially trained Tia primary care provider
A plan for next steps if we find something that needs attention, from monitoring to treatment or referral.
Personalized guidance on protecting your skin and performing self-checks at home to support early detection

children.text: Takes 3–5 minutes to create an account • No membership fee to book
Already a Tia member? Book here
What is this appointment?
A Yearly Skin Exam is an in-clinic visit with a specially trained Tia primary care provider to check your skin from head to toe, spot any changes early, and support your long-term skin health.
Who should get a Yearly Skin Exam?

A Yearly Skin Exam is for anyone who wants to take care of their skin and catch changes early. Regular checks are one of the best ways to prevent skin cancer from going undetected. This visit may be especially important if you:
Have fair skin, freckles, or a history of sunburns or tanning
Notice new or changing moles, spots, or patches on your skin
Have a family history of basal cell carcinoma and squamous cell carcinoma or precancerous growths
Spend a lot of time outdoors or have high sun exposure
Symptoms we care for:
During your Yearly Skin Exam, your provider will pay close attention to skin changes such as:
New or unusual moles, spots, or patches
Growths that are changing in size, shape, or color
Lesions that are itchy, painful, bleeding, or not healing
Rough, scaly patches (which can be precancerous)
How the Yearly Skin Exam can help:
Early detection:
Spot skin changes early, when they’re easiest to treat
Comprehensive evaluation:
Receive a thorough, head-to-toe exam from a trained provider
Personalized guidance:
Learn how to protect your skin and check for changes at home
Next steps if needed:
Access follow-up care, such as a biopsy or dermatologist referral
Your Yearly Skin Exam: What to Expect
Step 1
Arrive for your in-clinic exam with a Tia primary care provider (MD, NP, or PA).
Step 2

Get a thorough head-to-toe skin check - including scalp, face, neck, arms, chest, back, abdomen, legs, feet, and even between your toes. If your provider finds anything they want to get more information about, they may recommend a biopsy (sometimes done on the spot) or a referral to a dermatologist.
Step 3
Leave with personalized education on self-checks and skin protection. Plan to return yearly (or sooner if you notice new or changing spots).
Insurance and Pricing
Is this covered by insurance?

Tia will submit this visit to your insurance.

It’s important to know that many plans don’t treat yearly skin exams as preventive care unless you have a specific concern. When that happens, the visit is billed like a regular office visit — which may mean a copay or some out-of-pocket costs depending on your plan. 

We recommend checking your specific benefits ahead of your visit.
Our experts in Yearly Skin Exams
Where to get an appointment
Book an appointment

Taking care of your skin is key to staying healthy and catching changes early.
A yearly skin exam helps you check for moles, spots, or growths that may need attention.
Your provider will examine your skin from head to toe, answer your questions, and help you understand what’s normal and what’s not, so you can feel confident about your skin’s health year-round.

children.text: What is a skin care appointment?
An acne, rash, or skin change visit at Tia can be facilitated in clinic or virtually and is conducted by an MD or nurse pracitioner.
When should you schedule a skin care appointment?
It is recommended to schedule an appointment to discuss skin changes if:
Your symptoms are not responding to over the counter treatment.
Your skin changes are associated with the menstrual cycle.
Your symptoms are becoming bothersome or are worsening over time.
What to expect during a skin care appointment
Here’s what you can expect during a Tia dermatology appointment:
A comprehensive assessment:
Your provider will factor in your skin concerns, overall health, and well-being to create your personalized treatment plan.
A customized Care Plan:

Based on your assessment, your provider will work with you to develop a personalized treatment plan. This may include a combination of medications, lifestyle recommendations, holistic therapies like acupuncture, or other interventions aimed at optimizing your skin health.
Follow-up care:
Seeing changes in your skin can take time, and our team will be there every step of the way to make sure your Care Plan is working.

After your appointment, we'll provide clear guidance on next steps. Whether it's booking a follow-up, adjusting your treatment plan, or providing ongoing support, we'll ensure you get the comprehensive care you deserve.
Emotional support:

Skin concerns can bring up a lot of feelings, and we're here to listen, provide support, and put you at ease. At Tia, care is collaborative. You’re always welcome to ask questions, express concerns, or discuss any care preferences with your provider.
Potential treatment plans for skin issues
Once your provider has conducted a thorough medical exam and reviewed your medical history associated with your skin issues, there are several treatment options they might recommend:
Depending on your symptoms, your provider might recommend additional lab testing.
Your provider might recommened keeping a symptom journal to track when, how often, and for how long your symptoms appear.
Depending on symptoms and severity, ertain over the counter medication can be recommended or your provider will perscribe medication.
If your provider suspects that your symptoms are more severe, you might recieve a referral to a dermatology specialist.
Got skin issues? Join Tia and schedule an appointment virtualy or at the clinic nearest you!
Located in New York, Scottsdale, San Francisco, and Los Angeles.

children.text: Clinical Wrinkle Care

Feel comfortable in the skin you’re in. Experience the welcoming environment of a Tia clinic and work with a highly trained Tia provider on a customized injection plan using products like Botox or Xeomin to help you feel like your best you.
Takes 3–5 minutes to create an account • No membership fee to book
Already a Tia member? Book here
What is Clinical Wrinkle Care at Tia?
Clinical Wrinkle Care is the treatment of wrinkles and signs of aging skin through injectables like Botox and Xeomin, delivered by Tia’s licensed advanced practice clinicians (NPs & PAs).

These treatments work by temporarily relaxing the muscles that cause lines and wrinkles from repeated facial expressions—like frowning, squinting, or raising your eyebrows. This allows us to target areas in both the upper face (forehead lines, frown lines, eyebrow lift, crow's feet, bunny lines) and lower face (lip lines, masseters, lip flip, smile lift, dimpled chin).
With a whole-health approach, your provider creates a personalized plan to deliver natural-looking results that help you feel your best.
Who Can Benefit from Clinical Wrinkle Care? Here’s What to Look For
You’re noticing fine lines or wrinkles from repeated facial expressions
You want to smooth existing wrinkles or prevent new ones from forming
You’re looking for natural-looking results that still feel like you
You want care that considers your overall health, not just your skin
Insurance and Pricing
Is this covered by insurance?
This is a cosmetic service and therefore not covered by insurance.
If not, what is out of pocket cost?
The cost of wrinkle care treatments depends on the number of units of Botox or Xeomin used. Your provider will recommend the right dosage based on your unique goals and needs.

A simple plan for real results, inside and out
Step 1
Your Aesthetic Nurse Practitioner will work to understand your goals and partner with you to align on an injection plan.
Step 2

Step 3
After your treatment, your provider will talk you through after care instructions.

children.text: Skin Procedure

items: Treat specific skin concerns with an in-clinic procedure performed by a specially trained Tia primary care provider
Remove and test a small skin sample with a skin biopsy, or freeze and treat abnormal tissue with cryotherapy
Leave with a personalized care plan, clear aftercare instructions, and next steps for your skin health

children.text: Takes 3–5 minutes to create an account • No membership fee to book • Insurance accepted
Already a Tia member? Book here
What is this appointment?
An in-clinic visit for minor skin procedures, recommended when your provider sees a spot, mole, lesion, wart, or other growth that needs closer evaluation or treatment.
The procedure may include a biopsy (removing a small sample for lab testing) or cryotherapy (freezing abnormal tissue with liquid nitrogen)
This visit is focused on giving you answers, relief, and a clear plan for next steps in your skin health
Who should get a Skin Procedure?

This visit is for patients who have seen a Tia primary care provider for a skin concern and were recommended a procedure like a biopsy, cryotherapy, or treatment of a spot, lesion, wart, or precancerous growth.
Symptoms we care for
Following an evaluation of your skin, your Tia provider may recommend a Skin Procedure to address concerns like:
Suspicious moles or growths
Precancerous or abnormal lesions
Painful or bothersome warts or other skin changes
If your care requires more advanced procedures or specialized follow-up, your Tia provider will refer you to a dermatologist.
How the Skin Procedure can help:
Provides answers by testing suspicious spots for skin cancer or other conditions
Removes or treats bothersome warts, precancerous lesions, or other abnormal tissue quickly in-clinic
Ensures you leave with a personalized care plan and clear next steps for your skin health
Your Skin Procedure Appointment: What to Expect
Preparing for your appointment
Review your care plan to understand the next steps your Tia provider recommended for your skin procedure. No additional prep is needed.
During your appointment

This in-clinic procedure is usually about 30 minutes. Your Tia provider will explain the procedure and answer questions before starting. Depending on your specific skin concern, the visit may include a biopsy (numbing the area and removing a small sample for testing) or cryotherapy (using liquid nitrogen to freeze and treat abnormal tissue).ood.
After your appointment
Procedures usually require little to no downtime. Your provider will share aftercare guidance with you to help you care for the treated area at home.
If you had a biopsy, your provider will send your sample to the lab and will share results and next steps once they’re available (usually in 1-2 weeks).
Your care plan will be updated to reflect follow-up recommendations (which may include repeat treatment or referral to a dermatologist) and ongoing skin health support.
Insurance and Pricing
Is this covered by insurance?
Yes! Tia will bill your insurance for your visit. Just like at any doctor’s office, you may be responsible for a co-pay or coinsurance, depending on your health plan.

If you have a biopsy, the sample is sent to Quest Diagnostics. Quest will bill your insurance directly for the lab testing, and you may receive a bill for any remaining balance depending on your coverage.
Our experts in Skin Procedure
Book an appointment

This appointment gives you the opportunity to address skin concerns directly with your Tia provider. In about 30 minutes, your provider will explain the procedure, answer your questions, and perform any necessary treatment, such as a biopsy or cryotherapy, to help keep your skin healthy and prevent future issues. Personalized care helps you feel confident and supported every step of the way.

main.shortDescription: Whether it’s a mole, spot, or wart that needs a closer look, your Tia provider can evaluate and treat it right in-clinic. This visit may include a biopsy or cryotherapy to give you answers, relief, and a clear plan for your ongoing skin health.

children.text: Microneedling

Improve skin texture, reduce scarring, and even out skin tone by stimulating collagen with microneedling. At Tia, we use the SkinPen® device, and highly trained providers who will help you reach your unique skin health goals. Regular sessions can help you achieve smoother, more even-toned skin.
Takes 3–5 minutes to create an account • No membership fee to book
Already a Tia member? Book here
How does microneedling work?

Microneedling uses ultra-fine needles to create hundreds to thousands of precise micro-injuries in the skin, triggering the body’s natural healing response. This process stimulates collagen and elastin production, promotes tissue remodeling without scarring, and delivers long-lasting rejuvenation. Most patients can resume normal activities within 24 hours.
What is SkinPen microneedling at Tia?

Microneedling with SkinPen is a cutting-edge skin rejuvenation treatment designed to refine skin texture, minimize wrinkles, reduce pore size, and diminish the appearance of scars. At Tia, we use the advanced SkinPen technology to stimulate collagen production and accelerate skin cell turnover, revealing a smoother, firmer, and more radiant complexion.
What can microneedling help with?
Microneedling can help address common skin concerns such as:
Acne scarring
Surgical scars
Enlarged pores
Wrinkles
Stretch marks
Signs of aging
Dull skin
Sun damage
Insurance and Pricing
Is this covered by insurance?
This is a cosmetic service and therefore not covered by insurance. See out of pocket costs below.
Microneedling: What to expect
Step 1

Patients arrive 30 minutes before their appointment to numb the treatment area. Your provider will review your skin goals, explain the procedure, cleanse your skin, and apply a topical numbing cream to ensure comfort.
Step 2

Once the numbing cream has taken effect, your provider will begin the microneedling treatment. Using the SkinPen device, they’ll gently glide over your skin for about 15 minutes. Most patients say it feels like a light vibration or gentle scratching.
Step 3

When the microneedling is finished, your provider will apply soothing serums or creams to help calm your skin. It’s common to notice some redness, warmth, or mild swelling (like a sunburn) for the first day or two.
Most patients resume normal activities right away, with any redness typically fading within 24–48 hours.
Our experts in Microneedling

children.text: Chemical Peel
Improve the texture and tone of your skin, reduce fine lines, and treat acne scars or discoloration with a medical-grade chemical peel, tailored to your skin type and goals.
Takes 3–5 minutes to create an account • No membership fee to book
Already a Tia member? Book here
What is a chemical peel and how does it work?

A chemical peel is a skin treatment where a chemical solution exfoliates the outer layers of skin, promoting new cell growth. Peels vary in depth (superficial, medium, or deep) and can be customized to your skin concerns.
Which peels does Tia offer?

Tia carefully selected a set of chemical peels to address a variety of skin care concerns on both the face and body. We use VI Chemical Peels because of their effectiveness, applicability across a wide range of skin concerns, comfort, and aftercare support.
VI Chemical Peel
Peel used: VI Peel Original
Improves skin texture, reduces fine lines, brightens tone; gentle yet effective
Good for signs of aging, rough texture, rosacea, first-time peelers, and sensitive skin
VI Chemical Peel Comprehensive
Peel used: VI Peel Precision Plus or VI Peel Purify with Precision Plus
Targets pigment and acne; reveals clearer, more even-toned skin
Good for sun damage, stubborn dark spots, melasma, acne, post-breakout marks, excessive oil production, post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation
VI Chemical Peel for Body
Peel used: VI Body Peel
Smooths and renews body skin; fades discoloration and improves texture.
Good for body acne, pigmentation, scars, stretch marks. Suitable for most body parts and safe for all skin tones.
Who is it for?

Chemical peels are great if you’re looking for a gentle and customizable medical-grade treatment to refresh your skin, target stubborn discoloration, or support acne-prone skin. VI Peels are safe for most skin types, including skin of color.
What can chemical peels help with?
Chemical peels can help address common skin concerns on both the face and body:
Uneven tone and texture
Fine lines and early signs of aging
Acne and acne scarring
Hyperpigmentation, melasma, or sun damage
Dull or congested skin.
Insurance and Pricing
Is this covered by insurance?
This is a cosmetic service and therefore not covered by insurance. See below for cash prices.

VI Chemical Peel for Body - $500

VI Chemical Peel Comprehensive - $400
VI Chemical Peel for Body - $450
Chemical Peel: What to Expect
Step 1
Each treatment lasts around 15-20 minutes and begins with your provider reviewing your skin care needs and any relevant health history.
Step 2
Based on your goals and skin type, your provider will select a chemical peel formula and will apply the peel in layers.
Step 3

Your provider will give you a take-home kit with post-treatment instructions and skin care products for after-care. You may notice some flaking by day 2 and peeling by days 3–5. By day 6, the peeling will ease; by day 7, most patients have stopped peeling.

modules.title: Where you can get dedicated care

asset.altText: skin care consultation 1

children.text: The Problem

Your skin is the largest organ in your body, yet its care is often siloed and disconnected from your overall health. You may wait months for a dermatologist when your issue, like acne or eczema, could be managed sooner by a primary care team with specialized training. Plus, navigating the world of aesthetic treatments feels overwhelming when you just want trustworthy guidance on what’s right for
your
body.
Tia’s Solution

Tia is your home for whole-person skin health. Your skin is a window into your well-being. By connecting conditions like acne and eczema to deeper issues like hormones or stress, we help you treat the root cause. Your care is delivered by an experienced team of primary care and aesthetic providers trained in women’s health, medical dermatology, and aesthetics. This means you get seamless support for both medical skin concerns and aesthetic goals (like wrinkle care or peels) , all in one connected place, without judgment or upselling.
Takes 3–5 minutes to create an account • No membership fee to book • Insurance accepted

asset.altText: skin care consult medic visit

asset.originalFilename: skin care provider checking patient skin.webp

children.text: Tia’s Skin Care may be right for you if:
You want to feel comfortable and confident in your skin.
You're dealing with a chronic condition like acne, eczema, or rosacea that won't go away.
You've noticed suspicious moles or new, unexplained skin changes.
You're interested in Botox or chemical peels but need a safe, expert provider.
You have skin flare-ups and suspect stress, diet, or hormones are to blame.
You want a personalized skin routine that is guided by science, not just social media trends.
You need a provider to check your skin but don't want to wait months for a dermatologist.
Takes 3–5 minutes to create an account • No membership fee to book • Insurance accepted
Why Skin Care matters
Skin is a window into your whole health

Your skin is the largest organ in your body, and changes often reflect what's happening internally. Conditions like acne, rosacea, and eczema can be flares of deeper issues, including hormonal shifts (like those from PCOS or perimenopause) , stress, or gut health. At Tia, we look past the surface to treat the full picture, ensuring your skin health is never disconnected from the rest of your well-being.
You deserve care that connects the dots

Traditional, fragmented healthcare forces you to treat symptoms in isolation—a dermatologist for your rash, a gynecologist for your hormones, and a PCP for your stress. This disconnect often leads to band-aid fixes and frustration. Building a relationship with a single, connected care team at Tia means we understand how your sleep, mood, and cycle influence your skin, allowing us to find the root cause and build a unified, effective plan.
Skip the long waits and get trusted answers

Tia’s licensed providers are trained to evaluate and treat a wide range of common skin conditions, like acne, rashes, eczema, and more. Instead of enduring a month’s long waitlist for a specialist, you get timely access to medical and aesthetic treatments—from yearly skin exams to clinical wrinkle care—all delivered in a safe, judgment-free environment.

asset.altText: skin care procedures

children.text: How Skin Care at Tia is different:
Credible Skin Care, Connected to Whole Health:

Unlike Med-Spas or Derm Clinics, Tia integrates medical skin care (like treating acne, eczema, and rashes) with aesthetic services (like wrinkle care and peels) under one roof. Your skin plan works with your primary care, hormones, and overall well-being.
Expert-Led Aesthetic & Medical Care:

Our team includes Licensed medical providers who are specially trained in the services they deliver—from performing skin biopsies to administering neurotoxins, like Botox. This ensures safe, evidence-based treatments you can trust.
One Stop for Convenience and Confidence:

You get a full spectrum of services from an Annual Skin Check to Clinical Wrinkle Care, eliminating the need to visit multiple, disconnected providers. We create a personalized plan designed for confidence-boosting results.
Insurance Accepted for Medical Needs:

Medical dermatology appointments, like treating infections or performing cryotherapy, are billed to your insurance. You can get care for conditions like acne, rosacea, or rashes from your primary care team, helping you skip the long specialist wait times.
Takes 3–5 minutes to create an account • No membership fee to book • Insurance accepted

children.text: Book an appointment

Every stage of your skin’s journey deserves thoughtful, continuous care. Whether you’re dealing with acne, sensitivity, pigmentation, or aging concerns, Tia can help you understand what’s driving those changes and support you in feeling comfortable and confident in your skin.
At Tia, we've always believed that feeling good in your body is essential to overall health. This belief, deeply rooted in our
care philosophy

, has shaped every service we offer, from gynecology and primary care to mental health and acupuncture. We understand that true well-being is multifaceted and highly personal, so we provide a multidimensional approach to meet our patient's unique healthcare needs.
Today, we're thrilled to expand into a new dimension for our members by introducing
medical and aesthetic skin care treatments
Skin can be a barometer for your overall health.

Skin health is so much more than just an aesthetic concern. Skin can be a first health indicator for various health concerns. For example, dull, dry, or itchy skin can be signs of something going on below the surface. Issues like
acne, rosacea, and chronic rashes

aren't just skin-deep—they can be linked to stress, hormones, or diet or represent underlying complex health issues. Furthermore, how you feel about your skin can profoundly affect confidence, comfort, mental health, and quality of life.

At Tia, our approach to healthcare has always been integrative, offering a blend of treatments such as medication, acupuncture, diet and lifestyle recommendations, and mental health support to treat the whole person. Skin conditions like acne and rosacea are “whole body” healthcare issues, and caring for these conditions often should include recommendations beyond a single topical solution or medication.

With the addition of skin care in our primary care practice, we can treat conditions like acne, eczema, rosacea, and rashes with a holistic approach, just as we do with other health concerns. Skin health is so much more than skin-deep, and at Tia, we can treat both the frustrating and uncomfortable symptoms, as well as the underlying root causes.
Confidence and skin health go hand in hand.
In addition to medical skin care services, we're excited to introduce aesthetic skin care services like
wrinkle care
microneedling
, and medical-grade
chemical peels

. These services are frequently viewed as purely cosmetic, but we see them as another pathway to feeling good in your body. Just as primary care is vital to overall wellness, your well-being in your body (and skin) can profoundly impact self-esteem and mental health. Your skin is the presentation of yourself to the rest of the world, and when we don’t feel good about our skin, it can be hard to feel good about ourselves.

We recognize that each person's definition of "feeling good" is unique. For many women, that includes addressing fine lines, wrinkles, uneven skin tone, or scarring. For others, it might be about managing the physical discomfort of skin conditions like eczema. By offering medical and aesthetic skin care services, we offer women more choices in how they want to care for their bodies.
Not your average medical spa, our providers are licensed & trained.

The aesthetic skin care industry is generally not highly regulated, and it's common for practitioners to have limited medical training, depending on the type of care being provided. At Tia, we prioritize high standards of care, ensuring that our providers are thoroughly trained and qualified.

We've elected to use Aesthetic Nurse Practitioners and licensed providers with specialty training for our aesthetic services, ensuring our approach is clinically sound and evidence-based. Skin care at Tia (both medical and aesthetic) follows the same high-quality, empathetic standards we set for the rest of our services. Our skilled providers are here to help you put your best face forward.
Laugh lines or invisible pores, the choice is yours.

At Tia, our mission is to help you achieve your health goals while maximizing both your lifespan (how long you live) and your health span (the number of healthy, vibrant years you enjoy). We know the factors influencing aging are complex, but we believe aging should be celebrated. That's why we're committed to helping women find their version of “healthy,” whatever that means to them.

Whether you choose to embrace every line as a badge of pride, or opt for skin care solutions that help you maintain a youthful glow, we're here to celebrate you. Our providers know and understand this as well and will work with you to build a plan that’s as unique as your fingerprints. Our priority is to provide safe, evidence-based care that helps you to make the decisions for your specific journey.
Comprehensive healthcare from head to toe: body, mind, and, now, skin.

Ultimately, our mission is to give women the power to decide how they care for their bodies, minds, and selves. Whether managing chronic skin conditions or enhancing their sense of self through medical-grade aesthetic treatments, we support women in their journey to skin health.

Our expansion into skin care is a natural extension of our commitment to helping every woman feel good in her mind, body (and skin). With the addition of skin care services, we are reaffirming our commitment to comprehensive, integrative care that addresses both the body and the mind, inside and out.
Learn more about Tia's Skin Care services.

children.text: Hormones happen. And that’s a good thing. These chemical messengers, produced by various glands, are constantly working overtime to coordinate the most important functions in the body.
Metabolism. Sleep. Digestion.
Fertility
. Growth and development. Blood pressure. Sex drive.

It’s safe to say, hormones influence every aspect of human life. Unfortunately, these chemicals are exquisitely sensitive things, and just as hormones impact our lives, our lives can have a big impact on hormones too.
Hormonal acne is one condition that can develop when certain hormones fluctuate and throw off the delicate balance required to keep the body’s internal dance regulated and in rhythm.
What is hormonal acne?

As the name suggests (sometimes medical terminology makes so much sense), hormonal acne refers to breakouts that are influenced by changes in hormone levels. While any gender can have hormonal acne, regardless of age or skincare routine, it typically affects people assigned female at birth more than men. That’s because, you guessed it, women tend to experience more hormone fluctuations than men due to puberty, menstruation, pregnancy, and
menopause

Unlike the occasional pimple that pops up, hormonal acne can be persistent and can appear in the form of cysts and nodules (usually around the jawline, chin and lower face), which are deeper, more painful, and more likely to leave scars. This type of acne is most prevalent among women in their 20s and 30s, but it can affect teenagers and older women as well. In fact, according to the
International Journal of Women's Health
, up to 25% of women in their 40s develop hormonal acne.
Experiencing hormonal acne? Join Tia and schedule an appointment at the clinic nearest you!
Located in New York, Scottsdale, San Francisco, and Los Angeles.
What causes hormonal acne?
The short answer: Excess sebum in oil glands.

Hormonal acne is generally driven by fluctuations in estrogen, progesterone, and (especially) by androgens, which are a group of sex hormones (think testosterone) that play a key role in reproductive health and body development. During times of increased hormone production, the body makes more sebum, an oil/waxy substance that can clog pores and lead to inflammation, changes in skin cell activity, and bacteria colonization.

During certain life phases, like menstruation and menopause, estrogen decreases. This fluctuation can cause acne. During pregnancy and ovulation, progesterone increases, which triggers the release of sebum. This can also lead to skin problems.
Many women have excess androgens, particularly testosterone, and these hormones can stimulate oil glands and cause hormonal acne too. This explains why
people with PCOS
often experience breakouts of hormonal acne.
Of course, hormones are complicated, so it’s not always that simple. Medications, certain foods, anxiety, depression, and stress can also cause hormonal acne.
Preventing and Treating Hormonal Acne

Treating hormonal acne often requires a multi-faceted and flexible approach. There are some strategies to consider when planning your course of action to control hormonal acne, keeping in mind that trial and error, time, and patience are likely the most important factors when seeking a strategy that works for you and your unique situation:
Skincare:

Gently wash your face with a mild soap or cleanser that contains alpha hydroxy acid, like glycolic acid, or beta hydroxy acid, like benzoyl peroxide. These exfoliants help loosen dead skin cells, reduce inflammation, and prevent clogged pores.
Diet:

Some studies suggest that diet can influence acne. Foods with a high glycemic index (sugary, refined foods) and dairy products might exacerbate acne for some people. Adding more fruits, vegetables, and whole grains can be beneficial for your skin and overall health.
Stress Management:
Since stress can worsen acne, finding effective stress management techniques, such as exercise, meditation, or yoga might help reduce breakouts.
Medical Treatments:
If improved skincare and lifestyle changes aren't enough, it might be time to consult your healthcare provider for guidance about over-the-counter and prescription treatment options.
Topical Retinoids:
Most dermatologists consider this vitamin A derivative to be the mainstay of hormonal acne treatment. Retinoids can be harsh, so start slowly and wear sunscreen.
Birth Control Pills:
Oral contraceptives
, which help regulate hormones at the root of hormonal acne, can work wonders for people with this condition.
Anti-androgen Medications:

Medications, like spironolactone, block androgen receptors and decrease overall oil production. Despite its well-documented effectiveness, spironolactone can come with side effects, including irregular periods, headaches, and breast tenderness.
Isotretinoin (Accutane):
A form of vitamin A, isotretinoin is usually reserved for severe forms of acne. It’s a strong medication and requires close supervision to monitor for unwanted side effects.
Procedural Treatments:
In-office treatments, like laser and light therapies, can play a role in hormonal acne, but these options require a skilled dermatologist with experience with these methods.
Keep your breakouts at bay

Hormonal acne can be a challenge, but with the right mindset and approach, many people find success keeping breakouts at bay. If simple steps aren’t getting you there, talk to your healthcare provider about possible treatment options. Since other health conditions, like PCOS, can play a role in hormonal acne, your provider can also rule out any underlying issues that might be causing skin problems and hijacking your overall health and wellness.
It’s important to remember: A diagnosis of hormonal acne (or any condition) doesn’t define you and sometimes getting support and being seen is the most powerful medicine of all.
Need to see a doctor for hormonal acne? Join Tia and schedule an appointment at the clinic nearest you!
Located in New York, Scottsdale, San Francisco, and Los Angeles.

We’ll do a head-to-toe check to spot early warning signs, perform biopsies if needed, and give you peace of mind that your skin is being taken seriously.

faqs.question: Who should book an Annual Skin Exam?

children.text: Anybody who’s not sure where to start, wants expert guidance on their skin goals, or is curious about which treatments or services are right for them. A consultation is the best first step if you’re weighing options and want a personalized plan.

faqs.question: Who should book a Skin Care Consultation?

children.text: Yes. We treat acne, rashes, and more with prescriptions, procedures and integrative support. Our team evaluates what is driving your symptoms and create a personalized treatment plan.

faqs.question: Does Tia treat acne and rashes?

children.text: Most dermatology clinics or med-spas only focus on the surface. At Tia, we see your skin as a part of your whole health. That means, we connect skin changes to hormones, stress, and lifestyle factors. Your care is coordinated across one team, from primary care to aesthetics, so nothing gets missed.

faqs.question: What makes Tia’s skin care different?

children.text: Medical dermatology visits like Annual Skin Exam and procedures such as biopsies or cryotherapy are typically covered by insurance. Aesthetic services like Wrinkle Care, peels or red light therapy are elective and not covered.

faqs.question: Which skin care appointments are covered by insurance?

children.text: No, you will be seeing a Tia primary care provider (a Nurse Practitioner, Physician's Assistant, or Doctor) who has received specialized training in dermatology. They are equipped to handle many skin concerns. If your condition requires the care of a board-certified dermatologist, our team will provide a referral.

children.text: Consider acupuncture if you're:
Looking to support your hormones or hormone-related issues including PCOS, menopause, perimenopause, or cramping
Seeking relief from stress and/or support for anxiety or mood imbalances
Exploring alternatives for pain management for migraines, chronic pain, muscle soreness, or after a procedure like IUD insertion
Trying to conceive or seeking support on your pregnancy journey
Trust the science, the benefits are real
More than 2 out of 3 Tia members see improvement in their symptoms*

The intent of acupuncture is to bring the body back into a state of homeostasis and ignite the body’s innate ability to heal–and that can take some time. It often takes up to 6 treatments within a 3 month window to see significant results.
*Based on self reported data from N=120 Tia acupuncture patients who have had 6 or more appointments in the last 3 months.

children.text:
We’ve all, unfortunately, heard horror stories about IUD procedures. So at Tia, we offer IUD + Acupuncture to make your experience less *freaky* and more *comfy*.

This is just one of the ways we use multiple-tools-in-the-
toolkit — combining traditional modern medicine with evidence-based wellness practices to create an experience that keeps your comfort levels high.

Why acupuncture?

Acupuncture balances the nervous, immune, and musculoskeletal systems.

When paired with IUD procedures, acupuncture reduces uterine cramping, anxiety, nerves, and pain (by releasing endorphins).

Acupuncture is a highly effective, non-invasive pain relief aid that
makes the process of getting and removing an IUD less painful, and lessens discomfort afterward.
Did you know?

Acupuncture has been used for centuries to help reduce pain, both acute, and chronic. Its pain-relieving effects have been so powerful that in China, acupuncture has been successfully used in place of anesthesia during surgery!

main.shortDescription: We love this "set-it-and-forget-it" birth control method, but we know it can be painful to get inserted and removed. With acupuncture, reduce anxiety and pain before, during, and post procedure.

children.text: Red Light Therapy
Red Light Therapy helps enhance energy production & recovery, promote skin health, reduce pain & inflammation, and is easy, quick, & effective.
Takes 3–5 minutes to create an account • No membership fee to book
Already a Tia member? Book here
Is Red Light Therapy right for me?
What is Red Light Therapy?
Red Light Therapy (RLT) is a non-invasive treatment that enhances energy production & recovery, rejuvenates skin, relieves pain and inflammation, and much more
It works by targeting the mitochondria in the body’s cell (the cell’s energy source) and assisting in blood flow and cell regeneration.
It is quick, painless, and effective – making it easy to fit into your regular routine.
Who Can Benefit from Red Light Therapy? Here’s What to Look For
You are experiencing inflammation, joint pain, or deep muscle soreness
You want natural support for thyroid health, bone healing, or overall recovery
You are managing chronic skin conditions like acne, eczema, or dermatitis
You want to improve skin texture, tone, and appearance—reducing wrinkles, fine lines, acne, or scars
What to Expect
Step 1:
Choose a 20-minute appointment time that fits with your schedule.
Step 2:
Spend 5–15 minutes under a Hooga Red Light Therapy panel.
Step 3:
Leave feeling refreshed and rejuvenated.
Insurance and Pricing
Is this covered by insurance?
Red light therapy is not covered by insurance.
If not, what is out of pocket cost?
Each session is $20.

children.text: What to expect when you try Acupuncture at Tia
The Environment
Relax and let everything melt away. Acupuncture at Tia is done in a semi-private room surrounded by curtains and supported by calming sounds and mood lighting.
The Appointment

Your 60-minute session is divided into two parts: You'll spend the first 20-minutes with your provider discussing your symptoms, goals and progress. Once you’ve aligned, your provider will get into the treatment. You'll spend the remaining 40-minutes in a deep state of relaxation, while the needles do their work.
Check our price list.

children.text: Meet our Acupuncturists

When you come to Tia for acupuncture, your acupuncturist will work in collaboration with your primary care providers, OB/GYN, and therapist. From two-part appointments like IUD insertion and acupuncture to offering holistic support for fertility, we believe in connected care that treats the whole you.
Fertility, depression and anxiety, IBS

Alexia is an acupuncturist and herbalist who is wholeheartedly dedicated to supporting women in living their most self-aware lives with a clear mind, wild heart, and vibrant body. Intuitive and sensitive, she has cultivated specialties in women’s reproductive health, mental-emotional health and internal medicine. Alexia also applies movement, meditation and nutrition principles to engage the body’s innate healing power.
Fertility, Irregular Cycles, PCOS

modules.title: READ MORE IN OUR BLOG, THE TIA TAKE

main.position: Manager of Acupuncture, Los Angeles, She/her
main.quote: "Each patient’s journey is so unique, so I try to assist in navigating the right path in a holistic and integrative way. Collaborating allows for Tia’s patients to have full support while making fertility decisions."

children.text: Christina is an acupuncturist who works to provide her patients with a personalized approach to wellness through acupuncture, supplements & herbs, nutrition, lifestyle, and breath-work. Aware of the way emotions can naturally affect the body and its functioning, from sleep disruption to back pain & gastrointestinal issues, she leverages Chinese Medicine to offer patients of all ages & backgrounds an integrative treatment approach for physical and mental health.

main.title: Christina DuBois, L.Ac.

meta.metaTitle: Christina DuBois, L.Ac.

children.text: Stress and inflammation

I'm an acupuncturist experienced in promoting healthy living in our fast-paced, challenging city environment. Autoimmunity, stress, fatigue, gastrointestinal issues, physical pain, and fertility support are common conditions that I treat. I hold space with empathy, positivity, and love to share Traditional Chinese Medicine wisdom along with current integrative medicine research with my patients.

meta.metaTitle: Daphne Lim | Acupuncturist at Tia

children.text: Gabrielle is a Los Angeles-based Board Certified Acupuncturist, Herbalist, and Functional Medicine Practitioner. She is passionate about education around Traditional Chinese Medicine and is an advocate for body literacy and social and health equity for all gender. and identity spectrums . Gabrielle uses a whole body approach including nutrition, movement, breathwork/meditation, and herbal support alongside her acupuncture treatments to optimize well-being.
Inter-generational trauma, all stages of reproductive health, herbal medicine

modules.title: READ MORE IN OUR BLOG, THE TIA TAKE

children.text: Vignette is a doctor of Traditional Chinese Medicine. She graduated magna cum laude from Emperor’s College of Oriental Medicine before pursuing her doctorate degree from Five Branches University. She has spoken as a clinical educator at institutions such as Kaiser Bernard J. Tyson School of Medicine, Cancer Care Point, and Tia. She has over a decade of experience working with patients of all ages, especially LGBTQ+ and BIPOC individuals. She offers patients a safe place to explore the origins of disease and discomfort they are feeling. Through her training & expertise in Traditional Chinese Medicine, she hopes to be a touchstone for understanding the language the body uses to tell you what it needs.

meta.metaTitle: Vignette Ching, L.Ac.

children.text: Why our members love Tia

testimonials.Content: I had to undergo a procedure here, and I was initially terrified about the pain involved. Fortunately, Nurse Practitioners Julianne and Alex, along with Acupuncturist Erica, provided incredible support throughout the entire process. Their compassionate care, from medication for pain and anxiety relief to soothing acupuncture, made me feel safe and supported in the room. I truly appreciate the way they held my hand and guided me through what could have been a very difficult experience. It's incredible to feel safe with other women healthcare professionals. Thank you, team, for making this so much easier for me
testimonials.Title: Taylor L.

testimonials.Content: Tia has been an incredible resource for my health as a woman. It has been such a relief to work with a team of health care practitioners who understand women’s health and are committed to addressing things at the root cause. The combination of western and eastern medical practices has been life changing and it gives me peace of mind to have the majority of my healthcare needs addressed under one umbrella. Their team is so responsive and consistent and supportive. I specifically would like to recommend Paolo Chico at the Silver Lake location for acupuncture.
testimonials.Title: Hannah R.

testimonials.Content: I love the Tia Clinic!! Been going there mostly for group acupuncture and have consistently had an amazing experience. Group acupuncture appointments are super affordable—that alone has made my annual membership basically pay for itself. The space is unbelievably beautiful and well thought out, and I love the acupuncturist, Elana.
testimonials.Title: Katie S.

children.text: From a Chinese Medicine POV, acupuncture stimulates specific points on the body and reminds the body where to send extra-love. The hypothesis is that it triggers blood flow, which initiates an immune response that helps the body heal. A more Western theory is that certain acupuncture points stimulate nerves that then trigger the brain to release hormones that can reduce pain, inflammation, and relax the nervous system.
Here are a few additional facts about Acupuncture:
Acupuncture has a calming effect on the nervous system, taking our bodies from “fight or flight” to “rest and digest”

By reducing stress, acupuncture reduces inflammation (which is a trigger for ANY health concern), facilitates our bodies’ ability to heal, improves blood circulation, and makes us feel a resounding sense of calm.
Given acupunctures’ ability to decrease stress and inflammation, it is an incredible tool to help treat, alleviate and relieve most unpleasant and unwanted symptoms throughout the body

children.text: Yes! Acupuncture is safe and actually quite beneficial throughout all stages of pregnancy to help support you in feeling your best. Acupuncture has been known to help reduce morning sickness + nausea, support healthy energy levels, regulate digestion and help to reduce pain (back, low back, sciatic, hip, etc.) associated with pregnancy.

faqs.question: Can I get acupuncture if I'm pregnant?

children.text: Yes! Acupuncture can be used to support your body during the preconception phase (as well as throughout pregnancy!) through helping to regulate your menstrual cycle, support healthy blood flow to your reproductive organs, balancing hormones and reducing stress levels. There have been many studies showing how acupuncture has helped to increase odds of conception.

faqs.question: What else can acupuncture treat?

children.text: Here are some pro tips from our provider team: make sure to eat at least two hours before your session, stay hydrated, and wear loose-fitting, comfortable clothing.

faqs.question: What should I do before coming in for acupuncture?

children.text: The most simple answer to this question is usually not! Depending on the thickness of the skin on the areas of the body we are working on, you may feel the needles go in, but there should be no sharp or painful sensations. Once the needles are in, people describe feelings of warmth, tingling or heaviness where the needles have been inserted; these are all signs that blood flow is circulating. Believe it or not, most people actually fall asleep during their treatment.

faqs.question: Does acupuncture hurt?

children.text: After your session, allow your body time to restore. Make sure to drink plenty of water, avoid strenuous physical activity, and listen to your body! Take time to adjust back into your day. It can be normal to feel a little faint or signs of a headache. These sensations after your treatment, especially if you didn’t eat or drink anything before your acupuncture can be normal. We recommend drinking extra water and eating a protein rich snack after your appointment.

faqs.question: What should I do after my acupuncture treatment?

children.text: We've gotten used to quick fixes, but acupuncture is about balance. While every patient is different, the benefits and longer-term improvements from acupuncture take time (we see it often takes up to 6 treatments within 3-month window). You and your acupuncturist can design a treatment plan that is right for you and your goals.

faqs.question: How many sessions does it take to see benefits from acupuncture?

children.text: We generally recommend waiting at least 1 hour or so before jumping into a workout after acupuncture, especially if it’s high intensity. If you do, make sure to drink extra water and make sure you’re well fed.

faqs.question: Can I workout after acupuncture session?

children.text: Your acupuncturist will include your custom recommendation on your care plan, but for most, we recommend following up within the first week of your first session and continuing weekly sessions for 4-6 weeks.
With more acute concerns, like pain above a 5/10 pain scale or high anxiety, it may be recommended to come in 2 times a week until those initial concerns subside.

faqs.question: How often should I come back for Acupuncture?
